_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
**1. Energy Data Tracking and Metering Infrastructure**
The workspace must implement continuous, fuel-type-disaggregated energy tracking for all covered NYC facilities. LL97 emissions calculations require 12 months of verified consumption data for every fuel type (electricity, natural gas, district steam, fuel oil). For warehouse and cargo facilities — which feature high-bay lighting, dock door operations, and 24/7 climate control — energy stratification makes point-in-time utility bill collection insufficient; real-time or interval metering systems are strongly recommended to enable accurate monthly tracking and to detect compliance risk early. Scope: all NYC facilities ≥25,000 sq ft (or contributing to a 50,000 sq ft aggregate on a BBL). Gap from baseline: most freight/logistics facilities lack fuel-type submetering at the granularity required; utility automatic upload to ESPM should be configured. Deadline: data must be ready to support annual May 1 BEAM filing.

**2. ESPM / BEAM / DOB NOW System Integration**
The workspace must establish and maintain linked accounts in DOB NOW, EPA Energy Star Portfolio Manager, and the BEAM portal (nyc.beam-portal.org), using identical credentials across all three platforms. ESPM must be configured to share the building's property data with NYC automatically. As of 2026, ESPM property-type classification is mandatory for all reported buildings — a change from the transitional period (2024–2025) when original occupancy groups were still permitted. Gap: facilities that have not yet migrated to ESPM property types must do so before the 2026 reporting cycle (2025 data, due May 2026). Contact point: BEAM_LL97@buildings.nyc.gov for unlisted property types.

**3. Registered Design Professional (RDP) Engagement**
All annual LL97 compliance reports must be certified by a licensed PE or RA acting as RDP (or, in limited cases, a Retro-Commissioning Agent). The workspace must retain an RDP under contract sufficiently in advance of the May 1 filing deadline. For 2026 filings, the Dec 2025 extension for 2024-data reports was contingent on an RDP contract signed by February 1, 2025; for future cycles, RDP contracts should be in place no later than Q4 of the reporting year. Gap: workspaces without a standing RDP relationship face lead-time risk as demand for qualified professionals increases.

**4. Lease and Contract Clause Review**
For any NYC premises leased by the workspace in a covered building, leases should be reviewed for LL97 cost-allocation clauses. Landlords are increasingly inserting provisions allocating a share of emissions to individual tenants and passing excess emissions charges through as operating expenses. The workspace should negotiate for transparency in emissions measurement methodology, a dispute resolution mechanism specific to emissions allocations, and advance notice of retrofit capital expenditure pass-throughs. This applies especially to warehouse, art storage, and event equipment staging facilities in NYC. Deadline: at lease signing or renewal.

**5. Decarbonization Roadmap for 2030 Compliance**
Given that ~57% of covered buildings are projected to exceed the 2030–2034 caps (approximately 40–74% tighter than current limits depending on building type), the workspace should commission a multi-year decarbonization plan for each covered NYC facility. This plan should identify: current emissions baseline vs. 2030 cap; highest-impact retrofit measures (HVAC upgrades, LED lighting, building envelope, electrification of heat and hot water); eligible Tier 4 RECs or carbon offsets (up to 10% of reductions); phased timelines accounting for permitting and equipment lead times; and budget/financing options (NYC Accelerator offers free technical assistance and access to incentives). Construction timelines for major retrofits easily consume 3–4 years, making 2026 the practical planning deadline for 2030 readiness.

**6. Staff Training**
Personnel managing NYC facility operations must be trained on: LL97 covered building determination; annual BEAM reporting workflow; the distinction between LL84 (benchmarking), LL88 (lighting/submetering), and LL97 (emissions cap) obligations; and penalty structures. DOB and NYC Accelerator publish webinars, compliance guides, and step-by-step portal guides available at no cost.

*Source: NYC DOB LL97 Page, NYC DOB, March 2026. https://www.nyc.gov/site/buildings/codes/ll97-greenhouse-gas-emissions-reductions.page; NYC Accelerator LL97 Page, NYC Accelerator, 2026. https://accelerator.nyc/ll97; Envigilance LL97 Warehouse Guide, Envigilance, January 2026. https://envigilance.com/energy-monitoring/nyc-building-energy-laws/local-law-97/ll97-warehouse/*
